---
title: "URSSI Research Software Engineering Best Practices"
subtitle: "7/15/2026 DBMI RSE-CoP Meeting"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [dracula, custom.scss]
    incremental: true
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    footer: "URSSI RSE Best Practices — DBMI RSE-CoP"
author:
  - name: "Jess Murphy"
    email: "jessica.murphy@cuanschutz.edu"
  - name: "Sarah D. Slack"
    email: "sarah.slack@cuanschutz.edu"
---

## URSSI Summer School {.smaller}

- URSSI = US Research Software Sustainability Institute
- Credit for this presentation:
  [Summer School 2026](https://github.com/si2-urssi/summerschool-June2026)

- *Note that the workshop was python-focused - and so this presentation covers*
  *python-focused tools - but the principles are applicable to other languages.*

## Presentation Goals {.smaller}

+ **Review best practices as presented by URSSI**
+ **Discuss agreement/disagreement**
+ **Discuss implementation in practice**

## Managing Environments {.smaller}

- Don't install packages into global environment
- Python:
  - Mac/Linux: use virtual environments 
    - options: **built-in venv**, virtualenv, uv
    - options that also handle packaging & production: hatch, poetry, pipenv
  - Windows/projects with non-python dependencies: use Anaconda
    - options: miniforge, pixi, micromamba

::: {.callout-discussion}
Do you use one system for all projects, or select the best system for each
project?
:::

## Software Design & Modularity {.smaller}

- Three targets for simpler programs:
  1. **Modular:** break code into small pieces that can be understood
  separately
  2. **Reusable:** enable code to be maintained, reused, and extended
  3. **Foresight:** anticipate and design for how software will be used

## Software Design & Modularity {.smaller}

- Five core ideas:
  1. **Decomposable:** broken into modules
  2. **Composable:** can be reused
  3. **Understandable:** each module alone
  4. **Continuity:** requirement change affects few modules
  5. **Isolation:** error in a module does not affect others

- Suggested use of common patterns:
  - Abstract base classes for common interface across data sources
  - Factory pattern for choosing implementation of common interface
    - [Link to common python patterns](https://github.com/faif/python-patterns)

::: {.callout-discussion}
What are the barriers to applying these in your work?
:::

## Structuring Python Packages {.smaller}

<div style="margin-top: 1.5em; margin-bottom: 0.5em">
```text
mypackage/                # the project folder
├── pyproject.toml        # the one config file (packaging + tools)
└── src/
    └── mypackage/        # the package (importable)
        ├── __init.py__   # makes it a package; defines the public API
        └── module.py     # single .py file with definitions (functions, classes, ...)
```
</div>

- **TOML**: minimal configuration file format ([link](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/))
  - plain text file of `key = value` settings grouped under `[section]` headers
  - easy for humans to read and machines to parse

::: {.fragment .no-reveal}
::: {.callout-discussion}
Have you made a python package? If so, did you use a .toml file?
:::
:::

## Building Python Packages {.smaller}

<div style="margin-top: 0; padding-top: 0;">
![](images/build-pipeline.svg)
</div>

- **Building:** turning your human-readable source code into a tidy, installable bundle
  - a build **backend** (ex. `setuptools`) is the tool that *does* the building
  - a build **frontend** (ex. `pip` or `build`) is the tool *you run*, which calls the backend

::: {.fragment .no-reveal}
::: {.callout-discussion}
Which build backend have you used or do you recommend?
:::
:::

## Collaboration with Git/GitHub/Workflows {.smaller}


## Testing {.smaller}

- Write tests for expected outputs, module integration, edge cases
- Tests check new changes don't break existing code
- Tests can guide code refactoring by showing you the user experience
- Write mostly **unit**, some integration, and fewer functional tests

::: {.callout-discussion}
Do you write tests? How do you decide what to test?
:::

## Linting/Formatting/Type Checking {.smaller}

- **linting:** look for common problems in the code (unused variables, double
  imports, etc.)
- **formatting:** reformat code to a consistent style
- **type checking:** verifies types are consistent with code, if written with
  type hints

- Use `.pre-commit-config.yaml` to automatically enforce code style and
  formatting
  - For python: black (formatting), ruff (linting), mypy (type checking)
- Use `.pyproject.toml` to configure formatting and linting options

::: {.callout-discussion}
Which of these three tools do you use regularly, whether in python or
equivalents for a different language?
:::

## Continuous Integration {.smaller}

of testing and linting/formatting/type checking

- Use `.yml` file in `.github/workflows/` to run tests across OS and python
  versions & run linting/formatting/type checking through GitHub Actions
- Optionally add check of code coverage with `pytest-cov`


*TODO: is it worth showing .yml? and pre-commit file?*
*TODO: do we want to cover automated release/PyPI packaging here?*

## Peer Code Review {.smaller}

**Code review:**

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Contributor opens PR] --> B[Reviewer reviews & comments]
    B --> C[Contributor revises & replies]
    C --> B
    C --> D[Reviewer approves]
    D --> E[PR is merged]
```

+ Reviewer steps
  ([link to detailed checklist](https://arfc.github.io/manual/guides/pull_requests)):
  1. Read the PR description
  2. Read the documentation
  3. Look through the code
  4. Check the tests
  5. Consider the user
  6. Suggest constructive improvements
  7. Try to use the new feature

::: {.callout-discussion}
Is code review a regular part of your work? What are the barriers to
implementing it? Is this a good place for AI tools?
:::

## Documentation and Versioning {.smaller}

1. **README** — the front door: what, why, how to install
2. **Comments** — explain the why, never the obvious what
3. **Self-documenting code** — consistent PEP 8 names do the heavy lifting
4. **Docstrings** — runtime-accessible API docs; pick one style (NumPy/Google/RST)
5. **Sphinx** — turns docstrings + pages into a website
6. **GitHub Pages + Actions** — keep that site live and current
7. **Versioning (SemVer)** — `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` tells users what an update means

## Software Licenses {.smaller}

## Software Citation {.smaller}

## JOSS: Journal of Open Science Software {.smaller}

## Closing Discussion {.smaller}

+ **What do you agree with?**
+ **Disagree with?**
+ **What does implementation look like in practice?**
